data.title: Prologis: Entering A Phase Of Accelerated Earnings Growth
data.description:
Summary

I am positive on Prologis (PLD) because the business is entering a much more favorable phase of the cycle with multiple tailwinds lining up at the same time. Core logistics fundamentals are inflecting as vacancy comes down and pricing power starts to return. On top of that, PLD’s exposure to energy and data centers adds a new growth element. Combined with a strong balance sheet, I see a setup where earnings growth can reaccelerate.

Company overview

PLD owns, manages, and develops logistics real estate. It has ~1.3 billion square feet across 19 countries, and the strategy is to focus on land-constrained, high-demand markets near major cities like Los Angeles, Tokyo, and London. In the past, PLD mainly leased logistics facilities to customers, primarily in business-to-business [B2B] and retail/online fulfillment categories (think e-commerce). Today, the focus tilts toward expanding into digital infrastructure and energy solutions (think data centers and renewable energy).

Q4 2025 Earnings Review

Q4 2025 was an important quarter that made me turn bullish for PLD. Rental revenue performance was solid, coming in at $2.09 billion, up from $1.94 billion last year. The quality of this growth is what matters because it was driven by lease mark-to-market, which should’ve been a headwind given the softening rates. Despite that, PLD continues to roll older leases onto higher rates. For perspective, PLD’s portfolio mark-to-market is ~18%, and what that means is that this 18% will eventually show up in the revenue over time (i.e., 18% growth is almost guaranteed even if other parts of the portfolio stay flat). Topline performance translated nicely into core FFO (funds from operations), which came in at $1.42/share (or FY2025 core FFO of $5.86). For 2026, management is guiding for core FFO of $6.15 (the midpoint), which implies 4.9% y/y growth. I believe this guide is too conservative considering the solid tailwinds (discussed below), and there is room for upside as the year progresses.

Key bull case points

I believe the setup for PLD today is the strongest in recent history. There are two points that matter here; the first is the real estate cycle. For most of recent history, the logistics real estate industry sentiment had been weighed down by rising vacancy, but that seems to be ending soon. In Q4 2025, PLD saw net absorption across its US markets up by 26% sequentially to 59 million square feet, and more importantly, absorption exceeded new supply deliveries for the first time since 2022. This is also in line with Cushman & Wakefield findings that there is renewal demand momentum. My view is simple here, grounded by basic economic knowledge. When demand exceeds supply, vacancy has to go down (which indeed, was down 10 basis points to 7.4% in Q4 for PLD).

Combined with the fact that new deliveries are set to slow meaningfully, with industry supply expected to fall from ~200 million square feet in 2025 to 180 million square feet in 2026 (data from Q4 2025 earnings transcript), it creates a demand/supply imbalance dynamic, which means pricing bargaining power should shift back to PLD’s hands. This sets up a backdrop where PLD’s rent growth could surprise the market on the upside.

The second bullish point is the energy and data center opportunity. The AI infrastructure investment supercycle is driving up demand across the entire value chain, from semiconductor chips to energy generation and also to data centers. Every part of the value chain is important, but access to power is a major bottleneck today. You can have all the chips and customers ready, but without access to power, the data centers cannot start. In this sense, PLD is a direct beneficiary because it has expanded its power pipeline to 5.7 GW (either secured or in advanced stages of development). Management has given a sense of the magnitude of opportunity here, guiding for data centers to potentially represent ~40% of development starts in 2026. That is a sizeable mix, and it should come with much more attractive yields since hyperscalers value time-to-market more than cost.

So long as the world demand for AI continues to be strong, which I think it will, this opportunity set is almost “guaranteed” as well, especially since data centers used for inferencing have to be in proximity to end users. As I said above, PLD already controls the land near major cities, so it is in a very good position to capitalize on this tailwind. Over time, as the portfolio mix becomes more data center related, we should see a positive impact on valuation as well as the market shifts its valuation paradigm from PLD being a pure-play logistics REIT to one that has exposure to the high-growth data center market.

Balance sheet

Of course, one cannot discuss a REIT without touching on the balance sheet. Ultimately, this is a business that needs to invest a lot in CAPEX to create the underlying assets to drive earnings growth. A good thing for PLD is that it sits on ~$7.6 billion in liquidity with a weighted average debt cost of just 3.3% (this is less than the US 1-year treasury note, for comparison). What this implies is that PLD has a structural cost advantage in today’s environment. Because its cost of capital is lower, it is able to price more aggressively vs. smaller developers. The large amount of liquidity that it has also means that PLD can fund and ramp up the development of data centers very quickly, putting it in a more favorable position vs. sub-scale players that lack funding.

Valuation

The primary assumption I have in my model is that FFO/share growth will accelerate back to high single digits by FY28, anchored by the two major tailwinds I mentioned earlier. This shouldn’t be a high bar since PLD has achieved this historically even without the AI tailwinds today. Without assuming any changes to the multiple, at 20x forward P/FFO multiple, the share price should be worth ~$142 by FY27. Add on the two years of dividends (~3.2% yield), and I can bridge to a total return of 18% over two years.

Investment Risk

Some investors may still look at PLD as a dividend REIT play, and that makes it highly sensitive to interest rate volatility. If rates go up for whatever reasons, the dividend yield spread may become much less attractive, and that could weigh on the share price. The pivot to data center development is great but also carries risk. If development yields are delayed by power permitting issues, this could become a drag on near-term margins.

Conclusion

My positive view on PLD is because of the cyclical recovery and structural growth tailwinds. The logistics portfolio is benefiting from tightening supply and improving demand, while the data center and energy pipeline add a layer of structural growth to the equity story. With a low cost of capital and ample liquidity, PLD can invest through the cycle and capitalize on the current trends.

data.title: European Natural Gas Prices Set To Surge: 2 Energy Stocks Are Particularly Well-Positioned
data.description:
Investment thesis

European natural gas inventories crossed below the five-year average at the start of 2025, and as of now, the gap widened to over 20% below the five-year average. It is a gap that amounts to about 17 billion cubic meters, or more or less equivalent to the annual demand in a country like Belgium or Poland. It is an indication of a substantial supply/demand gap based on available supplies at current market prices. In other words, Europe needs to bid the price up much higher if it is to attract more supplies to close that gap.

I started buying Equinor (EQNR) stock at the start of last year, in response to the widening supply/demand gap that I followed starting in 2024, when the trend of supply shortfalls became evident in the data. As the inventory situation gradually worsened, with no market reaction to the data, I continued buying incrementally throughout 2025, making Equinor my third-largest position in my portfolio.

I bought Cheniere (LNG) shares with the same external factor in mind. However, I only considered this investment after a roughly 20% decline in its share price from its all-time high. In other words, it was a combination of a significant company-specific dip in its stock, coupled with an intensifying positive external situation, that the market failed to react to for over a year, as it developed.

Now that the market is finally starting to notice the European natural gas fundamentals, with the natural gas spot price moving higher and likely to persist in this direction going forward, I want to evaluate the dynamics of each investment choice to figure out which one is more likely to outperform. While both companies are deeply tied to the European natural gas market, their company profiles differ substantially. Additional factors and possible outcomes can determine which stock will outperform.

Maintaining a buy rating for both Equinor and Cheniere

I last covered Equinor a month ago, when the European natural gas market was inexplicably falling, even as storage levels continued to dwindle at a fast pace. Its share price is currently up about 6% since that article. I continue to see it as a buy, given that I foresee higher European natural gas prices for the foreseeable future, as low inventory levels will push European buyers to bid up the price for the rest of the year to make up for the current inventory shortfall.

Cheniere became a buy in my books recently after the sizable selloff in its share price, which I highlighted in my December article. Its share price is up about 8% since then, and it remains a buy on the same assumption of positive external trends, as is the case with Equinor.

An inexplicable year on European natural gas markets

As I watched European natural gas inventories slide from being far above the five-year average in the fall of 2024 to crossing that level, dipping below by the end of that year, I expected European natural gas prices to react.

At first, there was a minor market reaction. Then European natural gas prices inexplicably fell throughout most of last year, even as inventories steadily declined relative to the five-year average.

The most baffling defiance of market fundamentals by the market occurred at the start of this year, as inventory levels have been firmly stuck at levels that are the second lowest in the past six years.

If inventories continue along the current path, it is projected that by March, we will be below the technically dangerous 1,000 bcf level, where some countries will have to start rationing gas.

As we can see, not only are we headed for a deep dive in inventory levels, below the 1,000 Bcf threshold, where a crisis may start to emerge at a regional level, but we are also projected to see Europe entering the next heating season at barely over half-full inventory levels, which would put it on course for a deep energy crisis by the start of the 2026-2027 winter.

Recent events may compound Europe's crisis

There has been a significant upswing in the US Henry Hub spot price for natural gas.

The price spike is mostly weather-related, which may be transitory. If the upswing continues or if prices persist at current levels, there is a good chance that the Trump administration will opt to intervene by reducing US LNG exports.

US natural gas imports currently make up between a fifth and a quarter of all European imports. At the same time, most of the US exports currently go to Europe. If the US were to reduce exports even by a modest 10%, by the fall, it would have a negative impact of about 6 Bcm or about 190 Bcf, meaning that inventories might not even reach 50% full in September, unless other sources become available or if there is another downturn in European gas demand.

The recent news that Japan's largest nuclear power plant had to suspend operations after restarting it just recently may mean that Japan will have to import higher-than-expected volumes of LNG this year. The potential for unforeseen supply disruptions, such as a Ukrainian attack on the Turkstream pipeline, which is the last pipeline route that carries Russian gas to Europe, or conflicts affecting countries like Azerbaijan or Algeria, are also a real risk. A slight pickup in US natural gas demand or a slight decline in output might have an outsized impact on European supplies. These are all potential wildcard factors that may harm Europe's already precarious natural gas storage level situation this year.

Betting on a return of Russian gas

One possible explanation for why the market took so long to react to the worsening European natural gas situation is that there was some hope that a resolution to the Ukraine war would allow at least a limited return of Russian pipeline gas supplies to Europe. If this is what the markets have been betting on, it would provide a somewhat rational explanation for the lack of reaction to the trend for the past year. I personally doubt that this is the explanation, which is in some ways worrying, because in the absence of the rational, what is left is the irrational, or worse, some sort of public-private agreement that prompted companies to ignore the worsening storage level situation, until recently, when it can no longer be ignored and bidding on extra supplies at ever higher prices needs to take place.

The partial return of the Russian gas supply angle is, in my view, an unrealistic market expectation for this year, if anyone is inclined to trade based on this assumption. Even if there is a peace deal between Ukraine & Russia, it would be very hard for Europe to unwind the rhetoric that was employed to support the continued support of Ukraine in the war, which came with high direct and indirect fiscal & economic costs. The Russian military threat to the entire European continent was framed as being a near-certain existential threat to all European nations. Pivoting from that to the Russians riding to the rescue of Europe's economy through a resumption of gas deliveries during a time of crisis would be a very tough sell, prompting obvious questions about the prior rhetoric and its economic consequences.

We should also keep in mind that the Russians greatly diversified their energy export markets since the Ukraine crisis started in 2014. Exports of Russian natural gas to China rose in December to a new record high of 5.8 Bcm. On an annualized rate, that volume is equivalent to about half of all the gas that was delivered to the EU before the war. In other words, the Russians may not be as desperate to return to the European gas market as some might expect. It might demand some preconditions, such as the Europeans returning the massive central bank reserves that they froze. The EU used the proceeds from those funds to finance Ukraine, and the Russians might ask for those proceeds back as well.

Investment implications

Just in the past few days, the main European natural gas spot price started doing what I expected to see happen months ago, based on the unfolding fundamentals I described. The delayed market reaction arguably made the crisis worse, because higher market prices would have attracted more natural gas supplies over the past months, while now there is a higher gap, and a shorter timeframe for market forces to alleviate the situation. Fundamentals did not drive the market last year, but I think the market reached the point where it can no longer ignore those fundamentals. Low inventory levels are likely to keep European natural gas prices higher for at least the rest of the year, in order to avoid an even greater risk of physical shortfalls next winter than there is arguably toward the end of this winter.

Both Equinor and Cheniere are well-positioned to take advantage of this situation, and it is not entirely clear which one is more likely to outperform this year as a result of this all-important external factor. I am currently inclined with some degree of uncertainty to say that Cheniere will likely do better.

Equinor is Europe's largest natural gas provider. However, it is an integrated oil & gas company, with other business interests ranging from upstream, downstream oil interests, and windmills.

Cheniere, by contrast, is a company that is 100% exposed to LNG market trends, since it only produces LNG. If I am correct about the European natural gas market going higher this year, then Cheniere is in an obviously superior position to benefit relative to Equinor, since only part of Equinor's business will benefit from the same trend, versus 100% of Cheniere's business.

It should be noted, however, that Cheniere does have cost exposure to the surging US natural gas market price. It remains to be seen how the dynamics in the US market will play out in the next few weeks, as the expected demand surge from cooler weather plays out. It may be the case that US domestic natural gas prices will surge, in which case Cheniere can find that any LNG price gains will be wiped out by higher natural gas purchase prices.

The Trump administration may intervene out of political considerations by limiting LNG exports. Cheniere would then benefit from even higher European LNG prices, even as domestic natural gas prices would be kept in check. A prolonged cap on exports would potentially leave many of Cheniere's expansion projects in limbo if it is unable to expand LNG exports.

My Equinor position is currently significantly larger than my Cheniere position. I see it as being by far the safest option, with limited longer-term downside, since oil prices are currently at the minimum level needed to keep producers from dramatically pulling back on drilling. At the same time, the dividend, currently at 6% before Norway's special tax on foreign investors, makes this a good dividend investment, even if nothing else happens.

If my thesis of a sustained natural gas price rally in Europe turns out to be correct, there is arguably more upside for Cheniere, but if nothing much happens, Cheniere's dividend of 1% is not very enticing to keep investors interested in holding for a prolonged period, so there could be far more downside for this stock. LNG prices may surprise to the downside for many reasons. Cheniere also trades at a higher forward P/E ratio of over 12, compared with Equinor at 9, which arguably means that there is more potential downside.

Risks to my thesis

The idea that the Europeans could ever contemplate a pivot from framing Russia as their main threat to their very existence, to putting them in the position of saviors by resuming pipeline natural gas exports to the EU, may seem far-fetched. We do live in very unpredictable times; therefore, we should not entirely discount this possibility. Another severe energy crisis in Europe would be deeply damaging to the economy and to people's basic living standards; therefore, Europe's leadership may decide to act on a sudden rapprochement in the next few months. There is also always a chance that the weather will come to the rescue. The rest of the winter in Europe could turn out to be warm, sunny, and windy, creating the perfect conditions to cut natural gas demand.

Conclusions

At times, investors may get frustrated by a lack of market correlation to fundamentals. In this case, while at times I questioned my reasoning in the face of markets unexpectedly not reacting to ongoing fundamentals as expected, it turns out that the prolonged disconnect between fundamentals and Europe's natural gas markets was an opportunity to position myself for this moment. In the past week, it seems the markets finally gave up on ignoring the fundamentals.

Since I took up my initial position in Equinor stock in early February of last year, I made nine other additions to this stock in my portfolio, with my last purchase made in the middle of December. I also added Cheniere shares in two stages late last year, prompted by the selloff in its share price, even as the fundamentals were pointing to an ever more dire situation on the European gas markets. Based on recent European market moves, it looks like sticking with the fundamentals through last year, even as the market ignored them, is set to pay off this year, as the markets ran out of room to continue ignoring the facts.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Smurfit Westrock After Pricing News: Upside Looks Baked In
data.description:
Thesis

Smurfit Westrock Plc (SW) is a Dublin-based global leader in sustainable, paper-based packaging. The company's benefiting from renewed pricing power in containerboard as producers have cut about 10% of North American capacity, and Packaging Corporation of America’s (PKG) $70-per-ton price increase points to a tighter market. Even so, I’m staying neutral here. At this point, I think SW stock already prices in a lot of the good news.

The Setup for Pricing Power

PKG just announced a $70-per-ton increase effective March 1. After months of weak demand, too much inventory, and shrinking profit margins, it looks like the industry's trying once again to see how much it can raise prices. According to Philip Ng at Jefferies, it’s happening earlier than some people expected, but it lines up with the normal seasonal increase connected to agriculture and food shipments. More importantly, it’s happening at a time when a significant amount of capacity has already been taken out of the market.

Over the last year, containerboard producers have taken a sledgehammer to supply. About 10% of North American production capacity is now permanently gone, including roughly 3.9 million tons that were shut down just last year. It’s the most aggressive capacity reset the industry has seen in decades. International Paper (IP) and Georgia-Pacific (privately owned by Koch Industries) together shut down about 2.5 million tons of capacity last year. The impact's already showing up in the numbers. Per Morgan Stanley, even though demand's only growing at about 0.6%, production is being used much more fully, with operating rates nearing 93% overall and about 96% for kraftliner based on this year’s estimates.

For Smurfit Westrock, I think the timing here is hard to ignore. Coming off the merger, the company now has a much heavier North American tilt, with the region expected to account for roughly 64% of projected 2026 EBITDA. And this is exactly where pricing discipline is finally starting to return. Each dollar of higher pricing more directly turns into EBITDA, especially once volumes level out. We’re already seeing some early signs of that operating leverage. Last quarter, Smurfit put up $810 million of adjusted EBITDA in North America, which works out to a 17.2% margin. That’s an improvement versus earlier periods. And if prices hold and capacity utilization keeps tightening, then the earnings outlook heading into 2026 starts to look a lot more skewed to the upside.

That resilience isn’t totally accidental, but the result of a set of intentional and, I imagine, sometimes difficult decisions. Shutting down weaker facilities, like a corrugated plant in California, for example. And taking out about 500,000 tons of paper capacity across both containerboard and consumer grades. These improvements, along with a focus on value over volume that eliminated unprofitable business, put the company in a stronger position to benefit more from price increases than competitors that are less integrated.

With fewer assets and a more integrated way of operating, Smurfit is well positioned to benefit more than less integrated competitors if prices recover. Jefferies estimates that if the full $70-per-ton price increase is maintained across the industry, and assuming competitors like IP eventually follow, EBITDA could increase by 10–20%. For Smurfit Westrock, this could mean hundreds of millions of dollars in additional earnings, on top of the $400 million in run-rate synergies from the merger, which are already coming in higher than expected.

The amount of capacity they’ve taken out really changes the supply picture. And more often than not, it lasts longer than the market expects. Smurfit’s advantage in that environment comes down to scale and reach. They operate across EMEA, APAC, and Latin America. That's on top of North America, so the point is they can pull in global insights.

In Europe, where excess capacity is still a major issue, Smurfit’s mills operated at utilization levels in the mid-90% range last quarter and still achieved a 14.8% EBITDA margin, despite volatile input costs. Obviously, that speaks less to market conditions and more to operational control. Latin America also contributed, with margins above 21%, driven by export growth such as fruits and vegetables from Colombia, where volumes rose by 8%. I've highlighted these areas to show that having diversified sources of revenue helps protect the company when certain regions are weaker (overall though, the North American catalyst stands out).

Basically, the way it works is that by giving local managers responsibility for their own profit and loss, Smurfit cut the number of money-losing corrugated units in half within a year. This made 70% of its operations firmly profitable.

Looking out over the next 12–18 months, this pricing regime has the potential to carry Smurfit Westrock well into 2026 and beyond. Basically, if utilization stays where it is and demand picks up even a bit as the economy stabilizes, the company should be able to beat its $4.9–5.1 billion EBITDA guide for full-year 2025 without much strain.

Smurfit Westrock's Valuation

SW's at about a 17.6x blended P/E multiple on FAST Graphs. That sits between its long-term average of roughly 12.4x and higher forward estimates that already assume some upside. And a potential 10–20% EBITDA boost from higher containerboard pricing is already factored in margin expansion from the merger.

Earnings growth averaged a modest 3.65%. With pricing, merger synergies, and utilization all improving, earnings growth's now estimated at about 18.27%. Based on that outlook, fair value comes out to roughly $45.04 per share, hardly above the current share price.

Going into the February 11 earnings release, expectations are already high, with analysts forecasting 23.79% earnings-per-share growth in 2026. Weak demand could lead to more production downtime, increasing the risk of cash losses if fiber costs rise without any offsetting benefits, or even shareholder dilution if the company needs more debt and deleveraging stalls at 40.91% long-term debt-to-capital.

There's a bullish track (per FG above) in place for potential upside towards ~$74 if operating rates remain strong, although, competition could limit how far it goes. At an 18.06x forward non-GAAP P/E, I think we need to see the company beating expectations to justify that valuation.

Final Takeaway

Rating: Hold (with a bullish skew). The upside depends on pricing discipline staying in place and high utilization continuing through this year. Meanwhile, the downside risk comes from an already full valuation. Who should buy: Long-term, cycle-aware investors who are willing to be patient and believe containerboard pricing will hold and merger synergies will continue to exceed expectations. Who should avoid: Valuation-sensitive investors or short-term traders looking for quick gains from multiple expansion.
